questionText: Four sisters Suvarna, Tara, Uma and Vibha are playing a game such that the loser doubles the money of each of the other players from her share. They played four games and each sister lost one game in alphabetical order. At the end of the fourth game, each sister had â‚ą32. How much money did Suvarna start with?

steps.explanation: Work backwards from the end of the 4th game. After the 4th game (Vibha lost), each sister has â‚ą32. Total = â‚ą128.

Before the 4th game (Uma lost 3rd game; before 4th game Vibha loses): Reverse the doubling. When Vibha doubles others: each of S, T, U had their money halved (before doubling by Vibha). Before 4th game: S=16, T=16, U=16, Vibha=80 (since Vibha gave 3Ă—16=48 out: 128-48=80 before doubling).
steps.calculation: Before 4th game: S=16, T=16, U=16, V=80
steps.result: State after 3rd game: S=16, T=16, U=16, V=80

steps.explanation: Before the 3rd game (Uma loses): Reverse Uma's doubling. S, T, V each had their money halved. Before 3rd game: S=8, T=8, U=72, V=40.
steps.calculation: Before 3rd game: S=8, T=8, U=72, V=40 (Uma gave 3Ă—8=24 out: 72+24=96 before â†’ but check: 8+8+72+40=128 âś“; after Uma loses: 16+16+16+80=128 âś“)
steps.result: State after 2nd game: S=8, T=8, U=72, V=40

steps.explanation: Before the 2nd game (Tara loses): Reverse Tara's doubling. S, U, V each had their money halved. Before 2nd game: S=4, T=68, U=36, V=20.
steps.calculation: Before 2nd game: S=4, T=68, U=36, V=20 (check: 4+68+36+20=128 âś“; after Tara loses: 8+8+72+40=128 âś“)
steps.result: State after 1st game: S=4, T=68, U=36, V=20

steps.explanation: Before the 1st game (Suvarna loses): Reverse Suvarna's doubling. T, U, V each had their money halved. Starting amounts: T=34, U=18, V=10, and Suvarna = 128 - 34 - 18 - 10 = 66.

solution.shortcut: Work backwards from each game end, halving the amounts of those who were doubled each round.
solution.commonMistake: Trying to solve forward instead of backward; or forgetting the total money remains constant at â‚ą128.

formulas.title: Backward doubling reversal
formulas.expression: \text{If loser doubles each other's money, reverse: divide each non-loser's final share by 2 to get prior amount}

questionText: A team played 40 games in a season and won in 24 of them. What percent of games played did the team win?

steps.explanation: Identify the given data: total games played = 40, games won = 24.
Apply the percentage formula: Percentage of games won = (Games won / Total games) Ă— 100.

solution.commonMistake: Confusing games won with games lost, or computing (24/16)Ă—100.

questionText: At the start of a card game, J and B together had four times as much money as T, while T and B together had three times as much as J. At the end of the evening, J and B together had three times as much money as T, while T and B together had twice as much as J. B lost â‚ą200. What fraction of the total money did T have at the beginning of the game?

steps.explanation: Let total money = S. At the start: J + B = 4T, and J + B + T = S, so 4T + T = S, giving T = S/5.
steps.calculation: 4T + T = S â†’ 5T = S â†’ T = S/5
steps.result: T has 1/5 of total at start

steps.explanation: Also at start: T + B = 3J and J + T + B = S, so 3J + J = S â†’ J = S/4. Then B = S - S/5 - S/4 = S(1 - 1/5 - 1/4) = S(20-4-5)/20 = 11S/20.
steps.result: Initial: T = S/5, J = S/4, B = 11S/20

steps.explanation: At the end: J' + B' = 3T' and T' + B' = 2J', with J' + T' + B' = S. From J' + B' = 3T': 3T' + T' = S â†’ T' = S/4. From T' + B' = 2J': 2J' + J' = S â†’ J' = S/3. Then B' = S - S/4 - S/3 = S(12-3-4)/12 = 5S/12.
T's fraction of total at beginning = 1/5.

solution.shortcut: If J+B = 4T and total is S, then 5T = S, so T = S/5 immediately.
solution.commonMistake: Using the end-of-game conditions instead of beginning conditions for this question.

modelName: Fraction of Total Money â€” Card Game

formulas.title: Share from ratio
formulas.expression: \text{If } A + B = kC \text{ and } A+B+C = S,\text{ then } C = \frac{S}{k+1}

questionText: At the start of a card game, J and B together had four times as much money as T, while T and B together had three times as much as J. At the end of the evening, J and B together had three times as much money as T, while T and B together had twice as much as J. B lost â‚ą200. What fraction of the total money did J win or lose?

steps.explanation: From Q22 analysis: Initial J = S/4, Final J' = S/3. Let total S = 60x (LCM of 4 and 3 and 5). Then initial J = 15x, final J' = 20x.
Fraction of total that J won = 5x / 60x = 1/12. So J won 1/12 of the total money.

Verification: B lost â‚ą200. B_init = 11S/20 = 33x, B_final = 5S/12 = 25x. B lost 8x. If 8x = 200 then x = 25. J won 5Ă—25 = â‚ą125. T gained 15xâ†’T_init=12x=300, T_final=15x=375; T gained 3x=75. Check: J gained 125, T gained 75, B lost 200 â†’ net = 0 âś“

solution.shortcut: Express all shares in terms of total S = 60x to avoid fractions; compute change in J's share directly.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that the question asks for fraction of total (not fraction of J's own money).

questionText: In racing over a distance $d$ at uniform speed, A can beat B by 20 metres, B can beat C by 10 metres, and A can beat C by 28 metres. Then $d$, in metres, is

steps.explanation: Let the race distance = $d$ metres. When A runs $d$ metres, B runs $(d - 20)$ metres.
When B runs $d$ metres, C runs $(d - 10)$ metres. So when B runs $(d-20)$ metres, C runs $\frac{(d-10)}{d} \times (d-20)$ metres.
steps.calculation: C covered by the time A finishes = (d-10)(d-20)/d
steps.result: Distance C covers when A finishes = (d-10)(d-20)/d

steps.explanation: A beats C by 28 metres, so C runs $(d - 28)$ metres when A finishes. Set equal: $\frac{(d-10)(d-20)}{d} = d - 28$.

solution.shortcut: Express C's distance in terms of d using both B:C and A:B ratios, then equate to (d-28) and solve.
solution.commonMistake: Adding beats directly: 20+10â‰ 28 in general; correct approach uses proportional speed ratios.

formulas.title: Combined beat relationship
formulas.expression: \frac{(d - b_1)(d - b_2)}{d} = d - b_3 \text{ where } b_1, b_2, b_3 \text{ are beats}

questionText: A runs $1\frac{2}{3}$ times as fast as B. If A gives B a start of 80 m, how far must the winning post be from the starting point so that A and B might reach it at the same time?

steps.explanation: Speed of A is $1\frac{2}{3} = \frac{5}{3}$ times the speed of B. Let speed of B = $a$ m/s, so speed of A = $\frac{5a}{3}$ m/s.
Let the distance from start to winning post = $x$ metres. A covers $x$ metres, B covers $(x - 80)$ metres (due to 80 m head start given to B).
steps.calculation: Both reach at the same time

steps.explanation: Since they finish at the same time, the ratio of distances equals the ratio of speeds: $\frac{x}{x-80} = \frac{5}{3}$.

solution.shortcut: Distance ratio = speed ratio. Set $x / (x-80) = 5/3$ and solve directly.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that A runs x but B only runs (x-80) due to the head start.

modelName: Race with Head Start â€” Equal Finish
